locked_facts: Sound is a longitudinal wave — particle displacement parallel to propagation
Particles oscillate about mean position — they do NOT travel with wave
Compressions: regions of high pressure, particles close together
Rarefactions: regions of low pressure, particles spread apart
Speed of sound in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C, increases with temperature
truth_statements: Sound waves are longitudinal mechanical waves
In a longitudinal wave, particles vibrate along the direction of wave propagation
Sound requires a material medium for propagation
The speed of sound in air increases with temperature
Regions of high density are compressions and regions of low density are rarefactions

aha_moment.what_to_show: Particles vibrating in place while the compression pattern moves rightward. One tracked particle (orange) returns to its starting position after one cycle while the wave has advanced by one wavelength.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Tracked particle oscillates about its mean position (marked with a vertical line). The compression/rarefaction pattern moves rightward, but the particle stays in place. Particle does NOT travel with the wave.

simulation_states.label: Sound wave in air — particles at rest
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Row of air particles (blue dots) evenly spaced. No wave yet. Particles represent air molecules near a speaker.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Before the sound wave arrives, air particles are uniformly distributed.

simulation_states.label: Wave propagates — compressions and rarefactions
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Sound wave moving rightward. Regions where particles bunch together (C = compression) and spread apart (R = rarefaction). Pattern moves rightward.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Compressions = high pressure (particles close). Rarefactions = low pressure (particles far apart). The PATTERN of compressions moves along the medium.

simulation_states.label: Key insight — particles vibrate in place
simulation_states.what_student_sees: One particle tracked in orange. It vibrates back and forth about its mean position (marked). The wave pattern moves rightward, but the tracked particle stays near its original position.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The tracked particle does NOT travel from speaker to ear. It only vibrates about its mean position. The wave (energy) propagates, not the particles.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — adjust frequency
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider to change frequency. Higher frequency = more compressions per unit length (shorter λ). Particles still vibrate in place at all frequencies.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Changing frequency changes how closely spaced the compressions are (λ = v/f). The particles never travel with the wave regardless of frequency.

trigger_keywords: hawa speaker se kaan tak jaati hai
common_misconceptions.student_belief: Air particles travel from the speaker to the ear along with the sound wave
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: In a sound wave, air particles oscillate about their mean positions. They do NOT travel from speaker to ear. Only the wave energy propagates. If air particles actually moved, there would be a constant wind from every speaker.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Track one particle and show it vibrates in place while the compression pattern moves rightward. After one full cycle, the particle returns to its starting position.
